hypothesis: Momentum and MACD divergence signals capture early trend reversals; using ATR for dynamic stops adapts to volatility, improving risk‑reward balance while limiting losses in choppy markets.

When the momentum indicator rises above its zero line while the MACD line is also positive and momentum exceeds MACD, it signals a bullish trend that is likely to continue; conversely, a bearish alignment indicates a downtrend. Divergence between the two (momentum outpacing MACD) often precedes a reversal, so exiting when momentum crosses below MACD or becomes negative captures exhaustion. Using ATR to set stops and targets adapts risk to current volatility, improving risk‑reward consistency.
